column1: Artemis II Astronauts Fly Behind Moon’s Far Side Into Radio Blackout
The crew, which has temporarily lost radio contact, is expected to reach its farthest distance while seeing parts of the moon never seen with human eyes.
After a couple of flashes of blue as the signals from Orion became intermittent, the signal dropped entirely around 6:43 p.m. Eastern time.
As they were about to drop out of contact, Victor Glover said, “We’re still going to feel your love from Earth, and to all of you down there on Earth and around Earth, we love you from the moon.”
With most of the far side of the moon in darkness, the astronauts will not be able to describe the landscape below. Instead, they will look for flashes of meteors hitting the lunar surface.

Just before emerging from behind the moon, the astronauts will look for the glow of lofted dust, illuminated by light reflected from Earth. Once Earth comes back in view, it will be too bright to see any dust.

“I just had an overwhelming sense of being moved by looking at the moon,” said Christina Koch, the mission specialist of NASA aboard the space capsule. “Something just drew me in suddenly to the lunar landscape, and it became real.”
Only about 20 percent of the far side is currently in sunlight. When the astronauts pass behind the moon, most of the landscape below will be dark.
